subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Grounds for Divorce Under DA s.8 — Separation, Adultery, Cruelty
fact_pattern: Thomas and Sandra separated on January 1, 2025, and have not cohabited since that date. Thomas files for divorce on January 15, 2026, on the ground of marriage breakdown. He states they have lived separate and apart for more than one year. Sandra does not dispute the separation date. No other grounds are alleged.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act, has Thomas established a ground for divorce?

options.A: No — Thomas must also prove that the marriage is irretrievably broken by adducing evidence beyond the separation period.
options.B: Yes — under DA s.8(2)(a), living separate and apart for at least one year immediately preceding the determination of the divorce application constitutes proof of marriage breakdown, and the one-year period is satisfied.
options.C: No — the one-year separation period must be continuous with no resumption of cohabitation whatsoever, even for one day.
options.D: Yes — but Thomas must wait until two years of separation have elapsed before the divorce can be granted.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.8(1), marriage breakdown is the sole ground for divorce in Canada. Section 8(2)(a) provides that marriage breakdown is established by the spouses having lived separate and apart for at least one year immediately preceding the determination of the divorce application. Thomas and Sandra separated January 1, 2025, and the application is determined after January 1, 2026 — more than one year later. No additional proof of irretrievable breakdown is required beyond the separation period.
why_A_wrong: No additional proof of irretrievable breakdown beyond the one-year separation is required under DA s.8(2)(a). The one-year separation itself constitutes proof of marriage breakdown.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.8(2)(a) establishes marriage breakdown by proof of one year of living separate and apart, which Thomas has satisfied.
why_C_wrong: Under DA s.11(2), a period of resumed cohabitation of 90 days or less, for the purpose of reconciliation, does not interrupt the one-year separation period. Brief reconciliation attempts do not restart the clock, provided they do not exceed 90 days.
why_D_wrong: The statutory separation period for divorce in Canada is one year, not two years. There is no two-year requirement under the Divorce Act.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Grounds for Divorce — DA s.8
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 8(1), s. 8(2)(a)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Grounds for Divorce — Adultery and Physical or Mental Cruelty
fact_pattern: Natalie discovers her husband Adrian is having an affair. She wishes to obtain a divorce immediately without waiting one year. Her lawyer advises that adultery is a recognized ground for divorce that does not require a waiting period. Adrian has not admitted to the affair.
call_of_question: Which statement accurately describes the adultery ground for divorce under DA s.8(2)(b)?

options.A: Adultery is no longer a ground for divorce in Canada since the 2020 amendments to the Divorce Act.
options.B: Adultery is a ground for divorce under DA s.8(2)(b)(i); Natalie may allege adultery and, if proven, a divorce may be granted without the one-year separation period, but the standard of proof is balance of probabilities and Adrian cannot be compelled to testify against himself.
options.C: Adultery is a ground for divorce but requires a criminal conviction for the offence of adultery before it can be pleaded in divorce proceedings.
options.D: Adultery is a ground for divorce under the Divorce Act only if the responding spouse admits the affair in writing.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.8(2)(b)(i), adultery by the respondent constitutes marriage breakdown, which is a ground for divorce that does not require the one-year waiting period. The standard of proof is the civil standard — balance of probabilities — and corroborating evidence (direct or circumstantial) may be sufficient. The respondent cannot be compelled to testify against themselves as to adultery (DA s.10(4)). In practice, adultery cases are rarely litigated because the one-year separation ground is more straightforward.
why_A_wrong: Adultery remains a recognized ground for divorce under DA s.8(2)(b)(i). The 2019 Divorce Act amendments removed references to 'fault' in other areas but did not eliminate adultery as a ground for divorce.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Adultery under DA s.8(2)(b)(i) is a ground for immediate divorce; proof is on the balance of probabilities; the respondent cannot be compelled to testify.
why_C_wrong: No criminal conviction is required to establish adultery as a divorce ground. Adultery is proved on the civil standard of balance of probabilities in divorce proceedings, independently of any criminal proceedings.
why_D_wrong: A written admission is not required. Adultery may be proved by direct evidence or by circumstantial evidence sufficient to satisfy the balance of probabilities standard.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Grounds for Divorce — Adultery and Cruelty
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 8(2)(b)(i), s. 10(4)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Corollary Relief — Orders Available in Divorce Proceedings
fact_pattern: Karen and Paul are divorcing after a 10-year marriage with two children. In addition to the divorce itself, Karen seeks orders for parenting arrangements, child support, and spousal support within the divorce proceedings. Paul argues that property division cannot be addressed in the divorce proceedings and must be brought in a separate application under the Family Law Act.
call_of_question: Which of the following accurately describes the corollary relief available in divorce proceedings under the Divorce Act?

options.A: In divorce proceedings, the court may grant only the divorce itself; all ancillary matters such as support and parenting must be dealt with in separate Family Law Act proceedings.
options.B: The Divorce Act permits the court to make corollary orders for parenting arrangements, child support, and spousal support in the same proceedings as the divorce; property division under the Family Law Act is not available as corollary relief in federal divorce proceedings and must be pursued separately under provincial legislation.
options.C: The Divorce Act permits the court to make orders for all matters including property division, spousal support, child support, and parenting arrangements in a single proceeding.
options.D: Corollary relief is only available after the divorce has been granted; the parties must wait until the divorce is final before applying for support or parenting orders.

explanation: Under Divorce Act ss.15.1 (child support), 15.2 (spousal support), and 16 (parenting orders), a court may make corollary relief orders in conjunction with a divorce application. The Divorce Act does not confer jurisdiction over property division, which remains a matter of provincial law (Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I). Paul is correct that property equalization must be pursued under the FLA in a separate or concurrent provincial proceeding, not as corollary relief under the federal Divorce Act.
why_A_wrong: The Divorce Act expressly confers jurisdiction to grant corollary orders for parenting, child support, and spousal support in the same proceedings as the divorce. Karen's claims for these orders in the divorce proceeding are proper.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Divorce Act covers parenting, child support, and spousal support as corollary relief; property division is provincial and must be addressed under the FLA separately.
why_C_wrong: Property division is not available as corollary relief under the federal Divorce Act. It falls within provincial jurisdiction and must be pursued under the Family Law Act.
why_D_wrong: Corollary relief orders (for support and parenting) are regularly made at the same time as, or even before, the divorce itself. The court may make interim and final corollary orders without waiting for the divorce to become absolute.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Corollary Relief — DA ss.15.1, 15.2, 16
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), ss. 15.1, 15.2, 16; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Bars to Divorce Under DA s.11 — Collusion, Condonation, Connivance
fact_pattern: Gina and her husband Robert have agreed to allege adultery by Robert so that their divorce can be granted immediately without waiting the one-year separation period. Robert has not actually committed adultery, but both parties fabricate the allegation. The court is unaware of the arrangement.
call_of_question: What legal doctrine bars the divorce, and what is the court's obligation under the Divorce Act?

options.A: Condonation — because Gina has forgiven Robert's alleged adultery by continuing to live with him.
options.B: Connivance — because Gina has actively encouraged the alleged adulterous conduct.
options.C: Collusion — because the parties have agreed to fabricate or exaggerate the ground of divorce; under DA s.11(1)(b), the court must dismiss or stay the divorce petition if it finds collusion, and under DA s.11(1)(a), the court has a duty to satisfy itself that collusion has not occurred.
options.D: There is no bar; the parties' private agreement about how to plead the divorce cannot be reviewed by the court once proceedings are commenced.

explanation: Collusion under DA s.11(1)(b) is defined as an agreement, conspiracy, or arrangement to fabricate or suppress evidence or to deceive the court in relation to the divorce proceeding. Fabricating an adultery allegation to avoid the one-year separation period is a classic example of collusion. Under DA s.11(1)(a), the court has a duty to satisfy itself that there has been no collusion. If collusion is found, the court must (s.11(3)) stay or dismiss the divorce application. Collusion is an absolute bar — unlike condonation and connivance, the court may not exercise discretion to proceed despite collusion.
why_A_wrong: Condonation involves forgiving a proven matrimonial offence (such as actual adultery), typically by resuming cohabitation after learning of the offence. Here, the adultery is fabricated entirely, so condonation does not apply.
why_B_wrong: Connivance involves wilfully encouraging or acquiescing in the respondent's actual matrimonial offence. Since the adultery in this scenario did not actually occur, connivance does not apply.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Fabricating a divorce ground to deceive the court is collusion under DA s.11(1)(b), and the court has a mandatory duty under s.11(1)(a) to inquire into and refuse to grant a divorce if collusion exists.
why_D_wrong: The court has an independent, mandatory duty under DA s.11(1)(a) to satisfy itself that no collusion exists in relation to the application. Private arrangements between parties cannot bind the court or exempt proceedings from this scrutiny.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Bars to Divorce — DA s.11

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Jurisdiction — Ordinarily Resident for One Year Under DA s.3
fact_pattern: Abbas and Farida were married in Iran. They moved to Ontario in September 2024 and have lived there since. Abbas now wants to file a divorce petition in Ontario in October 2025. Farida has moved to British Columbia and has been living there since July 2025. Abbas's lawyer must advise whether an Ontario court has jurisdiction to hear the divorce application.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act, which province's court has jurisdiction to grant the divorce?

options.A: Neither province has jurisdiction because the parties were married outside Canada; a divorce must be obtained in the country of marriage.
options.B: Only British Columbia has jurisdiction because Farida is currently residing there, and divorce jurisdiction follows the respondent's province of residence.
options.C: Under DA s.3(1), a court in a province has jurisdiction if either spouse has been ordinarily resident in that province for at least one year immediately preceding the application; Abbas has been in Ontario since September 2024 — over one year by October 2025 — so Ontario has jurisdiction.
options.D: Both Ontario and British Columbia have jurisdiction because each party resides in one of those provinces; the parties may jointly choose which province to use.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.3(1), a court in a province has jurisdiction in divorce proceedings where either spouse has been ordinarily resident in that province for at least one year immediately preceding the commencement of the proceeding. Abbas has been ordinarily resident in Ontario since September 2024, satisfying the one-year requirement by October 2025. The place of marriage is irrelevant to Canadian divorce jurisdiction. While Farida is in BC, she has not yet been there for one year, so BC does not yet have jurisdiction based on her residence. Where both provinces would have concurrent jurisdiction, s.3(2) provides a mechanism to address conflicts, but on these facts only Ontario qualifies.
why_A_wrong: The place of marriage is irrelevant to Canadian divorce jurisdiction. Under DA s.3(1), jurisdiction is determined by ordinary residence in a Canadian province for one year — not by where the marriage took place.
why_B_wrong: Divorce jurisdiction under the DA is based on the ordinary residence of either spouse for one year, not on the respondent's province of residence. Abbas's residence in Ontario for over one year is sufficient to ground jurisdiction in Ontario.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.3(1) grants jurisdiction to the province where either spouse has been ordinarily resident for at least one year; Abbas satisfies this requirement in Ontario.
why_D_wrong: Concurrent jurisdiction arises under DA s.3 only where both spouses meet the one-year ordinary residence requirement in their respective provinces simultaneously. Farida has not been in BC for one year yet, so BC does not yet have jurisdiction.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Jurisdiction — Ordinarily Resident One Year DA s.3
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 3(1)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Joint Divorce Application — Uncontested Divorce Procedure
fact_pattern: Catherine and Raymond have been separated for 18 months. They agree on all issues — property, support, and parenting — and have signed a comprehensive separation agreement. Both want to obtain a divorce as quickly and inexpensively as possible. Their lawyers explain that in Ontario, there is a procedural mechanism specifically designed for couples in their circumstances.
call_of_question: What procedural mechanism is available under the Family Law Rules for Catherine and Raymond to obtain their divorce efficiently given that they agree on all issues?

options.A: They must each retain separate lawyers and each file separate divorce petitions; there is no joint procedure available.
options.B: They may use the joint application for divorce procedure under the Family Law Rules, where both spouses are co-applicants in a single proceeding and the matter can proceed on the basis of affidavit evidence without a court appearance where there are no contested issues.
options.C: They may obtain a divorce by filing their separation agreement with the court registrar without any judicial involvement.
options.D: An uncontested divorce still requires a full trial at which both parties must appear before a judge.

explanation: Ontario's Family Law Rules provide a joint application procedure for divorce where both spouses consent and agree on all issues. As co-applicants, both parties file the application together. In uncontested divorce proceedings, the matter can proceed on affidavit evidence without a court appearance, and is dealt with by way of a desk order (the judge reviews the materials in chambers). This procedure is faster and less expensive than a contested divorce. The court still scrutinizes the materials to ensure compliance with the Divorce Act (including the duty to consider reconciliation and bar requirements under DA s.11).
why_A_wrong: Ontario allows joint divorce applications where both spouses agree on all issues. Separate petitions are not required; a joint application proceeding is available and is the standard approach for uncontested divorces.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The joint application procedure under the Family Law Rules allows both spouses to apply together; in uncontested cases the divorce proceeds on affidavit evidence without a court appearance.
why_C_wrong: A divorce requires a court order; it cannot be obtained by merely filing a separation agreement. The court must grant the divorce, even if it does so on affidavit evidence without a court appearance in an uncontested proceeding.
why_D_wrong: Uncontested divorces do not require a trial or personal court appearance by the parties. They are routinely processed as desk orders where the judge reviews the affidavit materials without a hearing, provided all requirements are satisfied.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Uncontested Divorce — Joint Application Procedure
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99, r. 36; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 8

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Variation of Corollary Relief — Material Change Test Under DA s.17
fact_pattern: A divorce judgment was granted five years ago. The corollary relief order required Hamid to pay spousal support of $2,500/month indefinitely. Since then, Hamid has remarried and had two new children, and his expenses have increased significantly. He earns the same income as at the time of the order. He seeks a variation of the spousal support order, arguing that his new family obligations constitute a material change.
call_of_question: Does Hamid's remarriage and new family obligations constitute a material change warranting variation of the corollary relief order under DA s.17?

options.A: Yes — remarriage and new children automatically constitute a material change requiring immediate reduction of all pre-existing support obligations.
options.B: No — a payor's voluntary decision to remarry and have more children cannot constitute a material change because the financial consequences were entirely foreseeable.
options.C: Possibly — under DA s.17, a material change is one that, had it existed at the time of the original order, would have led to a different result; while a payor's new family obligations are a relevant change in circumstances, courts typically give less weight to voluntarily assumed new obligations; Hamid's unchanged income limits the persuasiveness of his variation application.
options.D: Yes — under DA s.17, any change in personal circumstances automatically constitutes a material change and the court must vary the order.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.17(4.1), a court may vary a spousal support order where there has been a change in the condition, means, needs, or other circumstances of either former spouse since the making of the order. The test for material change requires that the change, had it been known at the time, would likely have resulted in a different order (Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920). Courts do consider new family obligations as a relevant change but apply considerable scrutiny to voluntarily assumed new financial burdens. Because Hamid's income has not changed, and his new obligations were voluntarily incurred, courts are likely to give limited weight to this as a basis for reduction, particularly where the first family's interests may be prejudiced.
why_A_wrong: Remarriage and new children do not automatically reduce support obligations. The court must assess the material change test and exercise discretion; the first family's entitlement to support is not automatically subordinated to the payor's voluntary new obligations.
why_B_wrong: Foreseeability is a factor courts consider but does not categorically bar a variation application based on new family obligations. The material change test is broader than whether a change was foreseeable; courts weigh all circumstances.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. New family obligations may constitute a material change but courts weigh them carefully, particularly where income is unchanged and the obligations were voluntarily assumed; a variation is possible but not certain on these facts.
why_D_wrong: Not every change in personal circumstances constitutes a material change warranting variation. The change must be such that, had it been known at the time of the original order, the order would likely have been different. A mere change in personal life circumstances without impact on means or needs may be insufficient.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Variation of Corollary Relief — DA s.17
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17(4.1); Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Effect of Divorce on Property Rights
subtopic_heading: Divorce Act Does Not Divide Property — Provincial FLA Governs
fact_pattern: During divorce proceedings, Yvonne asks her lawyer whether the court can include an order dividing the marital home and investment accounts as part of the divorce judgment in the Ontario Superior Court of Justice. Her lawyer must advise her about the correct legal framework.
call_of_question: Can an Ontario court divide property between spouses as part of a divorce proceeding under the Divorce Act?

options.A: Yes — the Divorce Act gives Ontario courts complete authority to divide all property between spouses as part of the divorce judgment.
options.B: No — property division is exclusively a provincial matter governed by the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3; the Divorce Act does not give courts jurisdiction over property division; Yvonne must pursue equalization of net family property under the FLA in the same or a separate proceeding.
options.C: Yes — courts in divorce proceedings may divide property if both parties consent to the court making a property order.
options.D: No — property can only be divided by a separate Ontario Court of Justice proceeding; the Superior Court of Justice has no jurisdiction over property in divorce cases.

explanation: The Divorce Act is federal legislation and deals with corollary relief: spousal support (s.15.2), child support (s.15.1), and parenting orders (s.16). Property division is not included in the Divorce Act's corollary relief regime. Property division between spouses in Ontario is governed exclusively by the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I (equalization of net family property). While the same Superior Court of Justice judge may hear both the divorce and the FLA property claim simultaneously, the property claim is brought under the FLA, not the Divorce Act. The court's jurisdiction over property rests on provincial law, not the federal divorce statute.
why_A_wrong: The Divorce Act does not confer jurisdiction over property division. The corollary relief provisions in the DA (ss.15.1, 15.2, 16) cover support and parenting only. Property division is provincial, governed by the FLA.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Property division under FLA Part I is a provincial matter; the Divorce Act does not address property division; both claims may be heard concurrently by the same judge but under different statutes.
why_C_wrong: Consent does not give a court jurisdiction it does not otherwise possess. The Divorce Act does not grant jurisdiction over property division even with consent of the parties.
why_D_wrong: The Superior Court of Justice has jurisdiction over FLA property claims and may hear them at the same time as a divorce. The court's property jurisdiction comes from the FLA, not the Divorce Act. There is no rule limiting property claims to a separate Ontario Court of Justice proceeding.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Divorce Act Does Not Divide Property — Provincial FLA Governs
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), ss. 15.1, 15.2, 16; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I

tested_concepts: DA corollary relief limited to support and parenting

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Condonation as a Bar to Adultery-Based Divorce — DA s.11(1)(c)
fact_pattern: Nina discovered her husband Leo had an affair two years ago. After the discovery, she decided to try to save the marriage and they resumed cohabitation for eight months, though Nina remained deeply troubled by the affair. Ultimately, the reconciliation failed and Nina now seeks a divorce on the ground of adultery. Leo argues that Nina's return to cohabitation after learning of the affair constitutes condonation, which bars her from using adultery as the divorce ground.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act, what is condonation and does it bar Nina's divorce on the ground of adultery?

options.A: Condonation is not a recognized bar to divorce; Leo's argument has no legal basis.
options.B: Condonation under DA s.11(1)(c) occurs when a spouse forgives the other's matrimonial offence, typically evidenced by resuming cohabitation; however, the court has discretion under DA s.11(2) to still grant the divorce where the court is satisfied that it would be in the interests of public morality to do so.
options.C: Condonation under DA s.11(1)(c) operates as a bar to using the condoned offence as a ground for divorce; however, s.11(2) gives the court discretion to grant the divorce despite condonation if the court is satisfied it would not be contrary to the public interest to do so; a genuine failed reconciliation attempt may support the court exercising its discretion.
options.D: Condonation automatically bars the divorce and Nina must wait one year from the separation that followed the failed reconciliation before she can obtain a divorce.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.11(1)(c), condonation of the other spouse's adultery or cruelty is a circumstance the court must take into account; if the court finds condonation, it must satisfy itself whether it would be in the public interest to grant the divorce (s.11(2)). The court has discretion — condonation is not an absolute bar to divorce (unlike collusion, which is absolute). Nina's genuine attempt at reconciliation that ultimately failed may weigh in favour of granting the divorce on adultery, as the policy behind the bar is to discourage opportunistic use of a condoned offence, not to penalize genuine reconciliation efforts. Alternatively, Nina may simply wait and use the one-year separation ground.
why_A_wrong: Condonation is expressly recognized in DA s.11(1)(c) as a circumstance the court must consider when dealing with an adultery-based divorce. Leo's argument has a valid legal basis in the statute.
why_B_wrong: The court's discretion under DA s.11(2) is to grant the divorce if it is not contrary to the public interest — not to grant it only if it would promote public morality. Option B misstates the statutory test and the discretion standard.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.11(1)(c) recognizes condonation; s.11(2) gives the court discretion to grant the divorce despite condonation if it is not contrary to the public interest; a genuine reconciliation attempt supports exercise of that discretion.
why_D_wrong: Condonation does not automatically bar the divorce and does not impose a mandatory one-year waiting period. The court may grant the divorce on the adultery ground despite condonation in the exercise of its s.11(2) discretion. Nina could also choose to wait and proceed on the separation ground.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Bars to Divorce — Condonation DA s.11(1)(c)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 11(1)(c), s. 11(2)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Spousal Support Under the Divorce Act
subtopic_heading: Factors for Spousal Support Quantum Under DA s.15.2(4)
fact_pattern: A court has determined that a wife is entitled to compensatory spousal support. In determining the amount and duration of support, the court must consider the statutory factors listed in the Divorce Act. The husband argues that only the wife's current financial need is relevant; the wife argues that the court must consider a broader set of factors.
call_of_question: Under DA s.15.2(4), which factors must a court consider in determining the amount and duration of a spousal support order?

options.A: The court must consider only the financial needs of the claimant spouse.
options.B: The court must consider only the means of the payor spouse and the financial needs of the claimant.
options.C: Under DA s.15.2(4), the court must consider the condition, means, needs, and other circumstances of each spouse, including: the length of cohabitation; the functions performed by each spouse during cohabitation; and any order, agreement, or arrangement relating to support of either spouse.
options.D: The court must first apply the SSAG formula and may not consider any factors beyond the SSAG calculation.

explanation: Divorce Act s.15.2(4) requires the court to consider 'the condition, means, needs and other circumstances' of each spouse, and specifically directs the court to consider: (a) the length of time the spouses cohabited; (b) the functions performed by each spouse during cohabitation; and (c) any order, agreement, or arrangement relating to support of either spouse. These factors are broader than financial need alone. The SSAG are advisory tools that assist with quantum, but they do not replace the statutory factors in s.15.2(4). The court must engage with the statutory framework before (and in addition to) consulting the SSAG.
why_A_wrong: DA s.15.2(4) requires consideration of a broad range of factors beyond the claimant's financial needs. The statute expressly directs the court to consider length of cohabitation, functions performed, and existing support arrangements.
why_B_wrong: The statutory framework under DA s.15.2(4) requires consideration of the circumstances of both spouses and multiple additional factors. It is not limited to the payor's means and the claimant's needs.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.15.2(4) sets out a multi-factor framework including length of cohabitation, functions performed, and existing support arrangements, all of which must be considered in determining amount and duration.
why_D_wrong: The SSAG are advisory guidelines and do not replace the statutory framework. DA s.15.2(4) factors are the legal foundation; the SSAG assist with quantification but cannot displace judicial consideration of the statutory factors.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Spousal Support Factors — DA s.15.2(4)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(4)

tested_concepts: SSAG supplementary not replacing statutory factors

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Recipient Spouse Obligation to Achieve Self-Sufficiency — DA s.15.2(6)(d)
fact_pattern: Following a 6-year marriage with no children, a spousal support order is made in favour of Wendy, a 35-year-old with a nursing diploma. She stopped working during the marriage to accompany her husband on overseas assignments. It is now 3 years post-separation. She has not returned to work and has not sought employment, claiming the job market is difficult.
call_of_question: What obligation does the Divorce Act place on Wendy as the support recipient in relation to self-sufficiency?

options.A: Wendy has no obligation to seek employment or become self-sufficient; spousal support is payable as long as the original order is in place and is not varied.
options.B: Under DA s.15.2(6)(d), a support order must promote economic self-sufficiency of each spouse within a reasonable period, having regard to the circumstances; Wendy has an obligation to take reasonable steps to achieve self-sufficiency, and her failure to seek employment as a nurse may be grounds for reducing or terminating support.
options.C: Wendy must achieve complete financial independence within one year of the support order or the order automatically terminates.
options.D: Wendy's obligation to seek work is irrelevant because the support order is already in place; the court cannot consider self-sufficiency in a variation application.

explanation: Divorce Act s.15.2(6)(d) requires that a spousal support order 'insofar as practicable, promote the economic self-sufficiency of each spouse within a reasonable period of time.' This provision reflects the policy that spousal support is intended to address economic disadvantage, not to create permanent dependency. A recipient spouse with marketable skills (here, a nursing diploma) and who is of working age has an obligation to make reasonable efforts toward self-sufficiency. Courts may consider a recipient's failure to take reasonable steps toward employment when addressing duration and amount on a variation application under DA s.17.
why_A_wrong: DA s.15.2(6)(d) expressly imposes an obligation to promote economic self-sufficiency. A recipient's passive failure to seek employment when capable of working is a relevant factor that may justify variation or termination of support.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.15.2(6)(d) requires support orders to promote self-sufficiency within a reasonable period; Wendy's failure to seek employment when qualified may ground a variation application by her former husband.
why_C_wrong: There is no one-year automatic termination provision. The self-sufficiency obligation must be assessed in light of 'reasonable period' having regard to the circumstances; for a short marriage with a young qualified recipient, a shorter duration is likely appropriate.
why_D_wrong: Self-sufficiency remains relevant on variation applications. DA s.17 allows variation based on a material change, and the recipient's improved ability to achieve self-sufficiency (including failure to take steps toward it) is a recognized basis for a variation application.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Self-Sufficiency Obligation — DA s.15.2(6)(d)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)(d)

tested_concepts: variation based on failure to self-suffice

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Connivance as a Bar to Divorce — DA s.11(1)(b)
fact_pattern: Phillip secretly encouraged his wife Vera to begin an affair with a colleague by arranging for them to meet repeatedly and providing Vera with opportunities and privacy to be alone with the colleague. Vera did have the affair. Phillip now seeks a divorce on the ground of adultery.
call_of_question: What doctrine, if any, bars Phillip's divorce application on the ground of Vera's adultery?

options.A: Collusion — because Phillip arranged for the affair, constituting a fabrication of the divorce ground.
options.B: Condonation — because Phillip forgave Vera's adultery by continuing to live with her after learning of it.
options.C: Connivance under DA s.11(1)(b) — Phillip actively encouraged or wilfully allowed Vera's adultery, which bars him from using that adultery as the ground for divorce; the court must take connivance into account and determine whether it would be in the public interest to proceed.
options.D: No bar applies — the adultery is real and Phillip may rely on it regardless of how it came about.

explanation: Connivance under Divorce Act s.11(1)(b) refers to conduct by the petitioner that facilitates or encourages the respondent's adultery — such as wilfully allowing or setting up opportunities for the adultery to occur. Unlike collusion (fabrication of the ground), connivance involves actual adultery that was facilitated by the petitioning spouse. Phillip's deliberate arrangement of meetings and privacy to encourage the affair constitutes connivance. The court must take this into account under s.11(1)(b) and apply s.11(2) to determine whether it would be in the public interest to grant the divorce notwithstanding the connivance.
why_A_wrong: Collusion involves an agreement to fabricate or misrepresent the ground for divorce. Here, the adultery actually occurred — it was not fabricated. The concern is that Phillip encouraged and facilitated it, which is connivance, not collusion.
why_B_wrong: Condonation involves forgiving a known matrimonial offence, typically by resuming cohabitation after discovering it. The facts describe Phillip actively encouraging the affair before the fact, not forgiving it afterward — this is connivance, not condonation.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Connivance (DA s.11(1)(b)) applies where the petitioner wilfully encouraged or facilitated the respondent's adultery; the court must consider it and may exercise discretion under s.11(2).
why_D_wrong: DA s.11(1)(b) specifically addresses connivance as a circumstance the court must consider. A petitioner cannot rely on adultery that they wilfully facilitated without the court scrutinizing this under the bar provisions.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Bars to Divorce — Connivance DA s.11(1)(b)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 11(1)(b), s. 11(2)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Desk Order Divorce — Uncontested Divorce Without a Hearing
fact_pattern: Ahmed and Lily have been separated for 14 months. They have reached a full agreement on all issues — property, spousal support, and parenting — and have signed a comprehensive separation agreement. Neither party wants to attend court. Their lawyer explains that Ontario allows uncontested divorces to proceed without a court appearance.
call_of_question: What is a 'desk order divorce' and when is it available in Ontario?

options.A: A desk order divorce is a divorce granted by a court registrar without judicial involvement where both parties agree.
options.B: A desk order divorce is an uncontested divorce where the court grants the divorce on affidavit evidence reviewed by a judge in chambers, without requiring the parties to appear in court; it is available where there is no contested issue and the application is properly documented.
options.C: A desk order divorce is only available for marriages of less than five years.
options.D: A desk order divorce is available only in contested proceedings when the responding party fails to appear.

explanation: A desk order divorce (also called an uncontested divorce) is a procedure under Ontario's Family Law Rules (r. 36) by which a judge reviews the divorce materials in chambers without requiring the parties to appear in person. The applicant must file an affidavit confirming the separation, that all matters are resolved or addressed, and that there are no barriers to remarriage (DA s.21.1 obligation). A judge — not a registrar — reviews and grants the divorce. This procedure is available where the divorce is truly uncontested. It is faster and less costly than a contested proceeding.
why_A_wrong: A desk order divorce is granted by a judge reviewing affidavit materials in chambers, not by a court registrar without judicial involvement. Judicial involvement is required — only personal court attendance by the parties is waived.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A desk order divorce is an uncontested divorce granted by a judge on affidavit evidence without requiring the parties to appear, available where no issues are contested.
why_C_wrong: There is no restriction limiting desk order divorces to marriages of less than five years. The desk order procedure is available for any uncontested divorce regardless of the length of the marriage.
why_D_wrong: A desk order is not a default procedure for contested matters when the respondent fails to appear. It is a specific procedure for genuinely uncontested divorces where both parties agree and proper affidavit evidence is filed.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Uncontested Divorce — Desk Order Procedure
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99, r. 36; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 21.1

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Financial Disclosure Obligation in Divorce Proceedings — DA s.8(4)
fact_pattern: In divorce proceedings, Michel seeks spousal support and child support. His spouse Clara has not provided complete financial disclosure. Michel's lawyer advises that the Divorce Act imposes a mandatory financial disclosure obligation. Clara argues there is no obligation to disclose financial information unless specifically ordered by the court.
call_of_question: What is the financial disclosure obligation in divorce proceedings under the Divorce Act and the Family Law Rules, and what are the consequences of non-disclosure?

options.A: Financial disclosure in divorce proceedings is purely voluntary; courts may only compel disclosure upon a specific motion by the other party.
options.B: Under the Divorce Act and Family Law Rules, parties to divorce proceedings involving corollary relief (support or parenting) have a mandatory obligation to provide complete financial disclosure; failure to disclose may result in adverse inferences, cost sanctions, and the court drawing negative conclusions about the non-disclosing party's financial position.
options.C: Financial disclosure is required only if the court makes an explicit order; there is no standing obligation on parties.
options.D: Financial disclosure is required only for the payor of support, not for the recipient.

explanation: Financial disclosure is a foundational obligation in divorce proceedings involving corollary relief. The Family Law Rules (r. 13) require parties to serve and file a financial statement in prescribed form when seeking or responding to claims for support or property. The Divorce Act and the Federal Child Support Guidelines (s.21) impose specific obligations to provide annual income information. Failure to comply can result in the court drawing adverse inferences against the non-disclosing party, imposing costs sanctions, making orders on the best available information, or striking pleadings in egregious cases. Full and frank financial disclosure is a mandatory, ongoing obligation — not a matter of choice or court discretion.
why_A_wrong: Financial disclosure is not voluntary in divorce proceedings involving support or property claims. The Family Law Rules r. 13 and FCSG s.21 impose mandatory disclosure obligations. A motion to compel may be available but the obligation pre-exists any motion.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Financial disclosure is a mandatory obligation under the Family Law Rules and FCSG; consequences of non-disclosure include adverse inferences, cost orders, and negative findings about financial position.
why_C_wrong: No explicit court order is needed to trigger the financial disclosure obligation. The obligation arises automatically under the Family Law Rules when corollary relief claims are made. An order to compel may be sought to enforce the existing obligation, not to create it.
why_D_wrong: Financial disclosure is required of all parties to support or property proceedings — both payors and recipients. Both parties must disclose their full financial circumstances so the court can properly assess the appropriate order.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Financial Disclosure in Divorce Proceedings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99, r. 13; Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 21; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.)

tested_concepts: Family Law Rules r. 13 financial statement
applies to all parties not just payor

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Consolidation of Divorce Applications — Both Spouses File in Different Provinces
fact_pattern: Roberto files for divorce in Ontario, where he has been ordinarily resident for 18 months. Two weeks later, his spouse Maria files for divorce in British Columbia, where she has been ordinarily resident for 14 months. Both applications are now pending in their respective provinces. Roberto's Ontario lawyer must advise him on the procedure when both spouses have filed divorce applications in different provinces.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act, how is the situation resolved when both spouses have filed for divorce in different provinces?

options.A: The first petition filed takes priority; Roberto's Ontario petition prevails because he filed first.
options.B: Under DA s.3(3), where divorce proceedings are commenced on or after the same day in more than one province, or where actions are commenced in different provinces within a period of time, the court in the province that first acquired jurisdiction must determine the appropriate forum; typically, proceedings in the province where both spouses last habitually resided together are given priority.
options.C: Both courts proceed simultaneously and grant separate divorce orders; the parties may have two divorce orders.
options.D: Under DA s.3(2) and s.3(3), where proceedings are commenced in different provinces, a judge in one proceeding may transfer the other proceeding to their court, and proceedings in courts in both provinces are then consolidated in one court.

explanation: Divorce Act s.3(2) addresses concurrent jurisdiction when both spouses meet the one-year ordinary residence requirement in different provinces. Section 3(3) provides that where divorce proceedings are commenced in two different provinces on or after the same day (or close together), the proceedings shall be transferred to the court in the province where both spouses last habitually resided together — if that court has jurisdiction. If no such province applies, the court in the province where proceedings were first commenced has priority. Ultimately, the proceedings are consolidated in one court. Two simultaneous divorce orders from different provinces are not permitted, as there is only one marriage to dissolve.
why_A_wrong: The order in which petitions are filed is not determinative when both provinces meet the jurisdictional requirements under DA s.3(1). The Divorce Act provides a specific mechanism (s.3(2)-(3)) for consolidating concurrent proceedings, which does not automatically favour the first filer.
why_B_wrong: Option B incompletely and inaccurately describes the process. Under DA s.3(2)-(3), the proper approach is transfer and consolidation to one court — typically where both spouses last habitually resided — not merely identifying who 'goes first' among concurrent applications.
why_C_wrong: Two simultaneous divorce orders are not legally possible. There is only one marriage and it can be dissolved only once. Concurrent proceedings must be resolved by consolidation in one court under the DA s.3 framework.
why_D_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.3(2)-(3) provides for transfer and consolidation of proceedings in one court when both spouses have filed in different provinces, with priority typically given to the province of last habitual residence together.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Concurrent Proceedings — DA s.3(2)-(3)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 3(2), s. 3(3)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Post-Divorce Variation — Which Statute Governs Variation of Corollary Relief
fact_pattern: A spousal support order was made under the Divorce Act as corollary relief in a divorce judgment. Three years later, the recipient seeks to increase the support, arguing there has been a material change in circumstances. She files an application under the Family Law Act rather than under the Divorce Act. The payor argues she must proceed under the Divorce Act to vary a Divorce Act order.
call_of_question: Which statute governs the variation of a spousal support order that was made as corollary relief under the Divorce Act?

options.A: The recipient may proceed under either the Divorce Act or the Family Law Act, at her election, to vary a corollary relief order.
options.B: The Family Law Act governs all post-separation support variations in Ontario regardless of which statute the original order was made under.
options.C: Variation of a corollary relief order made under the Divorce Act is governed by Divorce Act s.17, not the Family Law Act; the application must be brought under the DA; DA s.17 requires proof of a material change in the condition, means, needs, or other circumstances of either former spouse since the original order.
options.D: No variation of a corollary relief order is possible; the original order is final and binding.

explanation: Divorce Act s.17 expressly governs the variation of corollary relief orders made under the Divorce Act — including spousal support orders under s.15.2, child support orders under s.15.1, and parenting orders under s.16. A party seeking to vary a DA corollary relief order must bring the application under DA s.17 and must prove a material change in the condition, means, needs, or other circumstances of either former spouse (for support orders) or the children (for parenting orders) since the making of the original order. The FLA cannot be used to vary a DA order. This is a critical procedural point for practising family lawyers: use the same statute as the original order for variation.
why_A_wrong: There is no election available between the Divorce Act and the Family Law Act for varying a corollary relief order. DA s.17 exclusively governs variation of DA corollary relief orders; the FLA is not available for this purpose.
why_B_wrong: The Family Law Act does not govern variation of orders made under the federal Divorce Act. The DA and FLA are separate statutory regimes, each governing variation of orders made under that statute. DA s.17 is the exclusive vehicle for varying DA corollary relief orders.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.17 governs variation of corollary relief orders; applicant must prove a material change in circumstances; the FLA is inapplicable for this purpose.
why_D_wrong: Corollary relief orders made under the Divorce Act are variational — they can be changed under DA s.17 upon proof of a material change in circumstances. The Divorce Act expressly provides this mechanism; orders are not permanently final.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Variation of Corollary Relief — DA s.17
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3

tested_concepts: DA s.17 governs variation of DA orders

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Willick Standard — Material Change Must Post-Date Original Order
fact_pattern: At the time of the original spousal support order, Nora was employed. Unknown to the court — and concealed by Nora — she had already been diagnosed with a serious illness that would affect her ability to work. Two years later, her health deteriorated significantly and she now seeks a variation to increase support, relying on the illness as the material change. The payor argues the illness cannot be a material change because it existed at the time of the original order.
call_of_question: Under the Willick standard for material change, must the alleged change have occurred after the original order was made?

options.A: The material change may have existed before the original order, provided the court was not aware of it at the time.
options.B: Under Willick v. Willick, [1994] 3 SCR 670, a material change must be one that: (a) could not have been reasonably anticipated at the time of the original order; and (b) if known at the time, would have led to a different order; a condition that existed at the time of the original order and was concealed by a party is not a new material change — the proper remedy may be to set aside the original order for non-disclosure, not variation.
options.C: Any change after the original order, however foreseeable, constitutes a material change and automatically entitles the applicant to variation.
options.D: The Willick standard applies only to parenting orders, not to spousal support orders.

explanation: In Willick v. Willick, [1994] 3 SCR 670 and Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed that a material change for variation purposes must be a change that: (1) was not within the reasonable contemplation of the parties at the time of the original order; and (2) if known at the time, would likely have led to a different order. Where the alleged 'change' — here, the illness — actually existed at the time of the original order but was not disclosed, it does not constitute a new post-order change. The appropriate remedy for the payor would be to seek to set aside the original order on the ground of material non-disclosure, not to resist a variation. If Nora concealed the illness, the original order may be vulnerable to being set aside entirely.
why_A_wrong: A pre-existing condition concealed from the court does not constitute a 'material change' for variation purposes. The change must be something that arose after the order was made. The court in Willick specifically held that the change must be one that was not within the reasonable contemplation of the parties at the time.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Willick requires the material change to be one not reasonably anticipated at the time; a pre-existing concealed condition is not a post-order change — the correct remedy is a motion to set aside for non-disclosure.
why_C_wrong: Not all post-order changes qualify as material changes. The change must be one that was not reasonably foreseeable at the time of the original order and that would have led to a different order if known. Foreseeable changes that were contemplated by the original order may not be 'material.'
why_D_wrong: The Willick standard applies to all corollary relief variation applications under DA s.17, including spousal support, child support, and parenting orders. It is not limited to parenting orders.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Material Change — Willick Standard
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Willick v. Willick, [1994] 3 SCR 670; Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920

tested_concepts: concealed pre-existing condition not material change
remedy is set aside not variation

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Interim Spousal Support in Divorce Proceedings — Test and Principles
fact_pattern: Bianca and Stefan are in contested divorce proceedings that are expected to take 18 months to resolve. Bianca left her part-time employment to care for their children during the marriage. She has few immediate resources. She brings a motion for interim spousal support pending the trial. Stefan argues that interim support should not be granted because Bianca has not yet established her full entitlement at trial.
call_of_question: What is the legal standard governing interim spousal support in divorce proceedings?

options.A: Interim spousal support cannot be granted before trial; entitlement must be fully established at a final hearing first.
options.B: Interim spousal support on a divorce application is governed by a summary assessment of the evidence; the court does not conduct a full assessment of entitlement but looks at the apparent or prima facie entitlement and immediate need; the purpose is to prevent serious hardship during the period before final determination.
options.C: Interim spousal support is granted as of right whenever the parties have been separated for more than six months.
options.D: Interim support is set at exactly 50% of the SSAG range and is not subject to the court's discretion.

explanation: Interim spousal support orders are available under the Divorce Act (and FLA for non-divorce proceedings) pending the final determination of the application. Courts assess interim support on a summary basis — without a full trial — looking at: (1) an apparent or prima facie entitlement based on the available evidence; (2) the immediate financial needs of the claimant and the ability of the other party to pay; and (3) the need to avoid undue hardship pending the final order. The SSAG may be used as a reference point for quantum. Interim orders are explicitly understood to be temporary and may be adjusted at trial; the court does not resolve all disputed facts at the interim stage.
why_A_wrong: Interim spousal support may be granted before trial, and indeed that is its very purpose — to provide financial assistance during the litigation period. Requiring full entitlement to be established at trial before any support is payable would cause serious hardship to claimants.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Interim spousal support is granted on a summary assessment of prima facie entitlement and immediate need; it is designed to prevent hardship pending the final determination.
why_C_wrong: Interim support is not granted automatically after a fixed period of separation. The court assesses apparent entitlement and immediate need on the facts of each case; the duration of separation is not a triggering threshold.
why_D_wrong: Interim support is not set at a fixed percentage of the SSAG range. The SSAG may be consulted as a reference tool, but the court exercises discretion based on the available evidence of need and ability to pay at the interim stage.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Interim Spousal Support in Divorce Proceedings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2; Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99

tested_concepts: prima facie entitlement and immediate need
SSAG as reference only at interim stage

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Jurisdiction to Grant Corollary Relief Before Divorce Is Absolute — Interim Orders
fact_pattern: A contested divorce application has been filed. The parties are disputing spousal support and parenting time. Trial is 14 months away. A junior lawyer advises the client that the court can only make support and parenting orders after the divorce is granted. A senior partner corrects this advice.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act, when may a court make orders for spousal support, child support, and parenting in a divorce proceeding?

options.A: The court may only grant corollary relief orders after the divorce has taken effect; interim orders for support or parenting cannot be made while the marriage subsists.
options.B: Under the Divorce Act, a court may make interim orders for support and parenting at any stage of the divorce proceedings — including before the divorce itself is granted; the court may grant a divorce and corollary relief simultaneously, or grant corollary relief before or after the divorce.
options.C: Support and parenting orders can only be made once both parties have filed all their evidence for trial; no interim relief is available.
options.D: The court can make interim parenting orders before divorce but not interim support orders; support orders require the divorce to be granted first.

explanation: The Divorce Act (ss.15.1, 15.2, 16) expressly permits the court to make interim and final orders for child support, spousal support, and parenting at any stage of the proceedings — before, during, or after the grant of the divorce. Interim orders under these sections are the standard mechanism for addressing immediate needs of the parties and children during the litigation period. The junior lawyer's advice is incorrect: the court has jurisdiction to make interim corollary relief orders while the divorce proceeding is pending, regardless of whether the divorce has been granted yet. The marriage subsisting is not a bar to an interim support or parenting order in divorce proceedings.
why_A_wrong: This is incorrect. The Divorce Act expressly provides for interim orders in divorce proceedings before the divorce takes effect. DA ss.15.1(2), 15.2(2), and 16(1) all authorize the court to make interim orders at any stage of the proceedings.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The DA authorizes interim and final corollary relief orders at any stage of divorce proceedings — before, during, or after the grant of the divorce itself.
why_C_wrong: Interim relief does not require all evidence to be filed for trial. By definition, interim orders are granted at an early stage on available affidavit evidence, precisely to address needs before the matter proceeds to full determination.
why_D_wrong: Both interim parenting orders and interim support orders are available at any stage of divorce proceedings. There is no restriction limiting support orders to post-divorce. DA ss.15.1(2) and 15.2(2) expressly authorize interim support orders.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Interim Corollary Relief Orders — DA ss.15.1(2), 15.2(2), 16(1)

tested_concepts: interim orders available at any stage
not required to wait for divorce
marriage subsisting not a bar to interim order

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Effective Date and Finality of Divorce Decree — DA s.12
fact_pattern: A divorce judgment is granted by the Ontario Superior Court of Justice on March 1, 2026. Neither party appeals. Patrick wants to know when he is legally free to remarry.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act, when does the divorce take effect and when may Patrick legally remarry?

options.A: The divorce takes effect immediately upon the judge pronouncing the judgment on March 1, 2026; Patrick may remarry that day.
options.B: The divorce takes effect 31 days after the date of the judgment (April 1, 2026) unless an appeal is filed; after that date Patrick may remarry.
options.C: The divorce takes effect one year after the judgment; Patrick must wait until March 1, 2027 to remarry.
options.D: The divorce takes effect only upon issuance of a divorce certificate by the court registrar; Patrick must apply for the certificate before he can remarry.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.12(1), a divorce takes effect on the thirty-first day after the day on which the judgment granting the divorce is rendered, unless an appeal has been taken. If no appeal is filed within 30 days, the divorce becomes absolute on day 31. Section 14 provides that after the divorce takes effect, the parties are free to remarry. The 31-day period is designed to allow for the filing of an appeal. A divorce certificate may be issued upon request but is not a prerequisite for remarriage after the effective date.
why_A_wrong: The divorce does not take effect immediately upon pronouncement. Divorce Act s.12(1) provides a mandatory 31-day waiting period before the divorce becomes absolute, designed to allow time for an appeal.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under DA s.12(1), the divorce takes effect on the 31st day after judgment if no appeal is filed, and the parties are then free to remarry under DA s.14.
why_C_wrong: There is no one-year waiting period after judgment. The Divorce Act prescribes a 31-day period from the date of judgment, not one year.
why_D_wrong: A divorce certificate is evidence of the divorce but is not a prerequisite for remarriage. Once the 31-day period passes without an appeal, the divorce is absolute and remarriage is permitted without a certificate.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Effective Date of Divorce — DA s.12
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 12(1), s. 14
